section.title: Second derivatives and the Hessian matrix
section.p: ended at critical points. At those points, df=\mathbf 0, so the first-order approximation stops talking. If a point is a peak, a valley, or a saddle, that information must come from the next visible part of the function.
That next part is second order.

subsection.title: The summit we already met
subsection.p: Return to the hill from and : h(x,y)=100+4x+3y-x^2-xy-y^2. Its critical point is P=\left(\frac53,\frac23\right). In , we wrote x=\frac53+u, \qquad y=\frac23+v, and found the exact formula h\left(\frac53+u,\frac23+v\right)=\frac{313}{3}-(u^2+uv+v^2). There is no linear part. The first visible change is -(u^2+uv+v^2).

That expression tells the shape. Since u^2+uv+v^2=\left(u+\frac12v\right)^2+\frac34v^2, it is positive unless u=v=0. So every nonzero nearby displacement lowers the height. The critical point is a local maximum.

Now look at the second derivatives: h_x=4-2x-y, \qquad h_y=3-x-2y. Differentiating again gives h_{xx}=-2, \qquad h_{xy}=-1, \qquad h_{yx}=-1, \qquad h_{yy}=-2. These four numbers arrange themselves into the matrix
H_h= \begin{pmatrix} -2 \amp -1\\ -1 \amp -2 \end{pmatrix}.
Then
\frac12 \begin{pmatrix}u\amp v\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -2 \amp -1\\ -1 \amp -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}u\\v\end{pmatrix} = -(u^2+uv+v^2).
So the second-order part of the hill is stored in a matrix.
That matrix is called the Hessian.

subsection.title: Second partial derivatives
subsection.p: A first partial derivative measures a coordinate slope. A second partial derivative measures how that coordinate slope changes.

For a function f(x,y), we have four second partial derivatives: f_{xx}, \qquad f_{xy}, \qquad f_{yx}, \qquad f_{yy}. The notation means f_{xy}=\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(f_x\right). So f_{xy} means: first differentiate with respect to x, then differentiate the result with respect to y.

statement.p: Let f:D\subseteq\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R. The second partial derivatives of f are f_{xx}=(f_x)_x, \qquad f_{xy}=(f_x)_y, \qquad f_{yx}=(f_y)_x, \qquad f_{yy}=(f_y)_y, when these derivatives exist.
Recall the bakery cost model C(u,v)=400+3u+4v+0.01uv, where u is the number of sourdough loaves and v is the number of rye loaves.
The first partial derivatives are C_u=3+0.01v, \qquad C_v=4+0.01u. Now differentiate again: C_{uu}=0, \qquad C_{uv}=0.01, and C_{vu}=0.01, \qquad C_{vv}=0.

The number C_{uv}=0.01 says that if the bakery makes one more rye loaf, then the marginal cost of making sourdough rises by 0.01 dollars. The interaction term 0.01uv is responsible. Without it, the two products would not affect each other's marginal costs.
Let T(x,y,z)=18+\frac{4}{1+x^2+y^2}-0.7z. Then T_z=-0.7, so T_{zz}=0. The temperature changes linearly with height, so the height-slope does not change as height changes.
For the x-direction, T_x=-\frac{8x}{(1+x^2+y^2)^2}. A second derivative such as T_{xy} measures how this x-slope changes as we move in the y-direction.

subsection.p: Second partial derivatives are rates of change of rates of change. They are the multivariable version of f''.

subsection.title: Mixed partials and why order usually does not matter
subsection.p: Two of the second partials are mixed: f_{xy} \qquad\text{and}\qquad f_{yx}. At first, they look like different operations. One changes x first and y second. The other changes y first and x second.
For ordinary smooth formulas, they are equal.

statement.p: Let f(x,y)=x^2y+3xy^2. Then f_x=2xy+3y^2, so f_{xy}=2x+6y. Also, f_y=x^2+6xy, so f_{yx}=2x+6y. Thus f_{xy}=f_{yx}.

subsection.p: This equality is not a coincidence. It is a theorem, but the theorem needs a regularity condition.

proof.title: Proof idea
proof.p:
Look at the small rectangle with corners (a,b),\quad (a+u,b),\quad (a,b+v),\quad (a+u,b+v). The quantity
\Delta \amp= f(a+u,b+v)-f(a+u,b) \amp\qquad -f(a,b+v)+f(a,b)
measures how much the change in one direction depends on moving in the other direction.

If we divide by the small signed area uv, \frac{\Delta}{uv}, we get an average mixed rate of change over the rectangle. The one-variable mean value theorem can read this average in two ways: as an average of f_{xy}, or as an average of f_{yx}. As the rectangle shrinks to (a,b), continuity forces both averages to approach the values at (a,b). Therefore the two values are equal.

subsection.p: The continuity assumption is not decoration. Without it, the two mixed partials can exist and still disagree.

example.title: Mixed partials can fail to agree

statement.p: So \boxed{F_{xy}(0,0)=-1, \qquad F_{yx}(0,0)=1.} The mixed partials exist, but they are not equal. The missing condition is continuity of the mixed partials near the origin.

subsection.p: In differential-form language, when f is smooth enough, df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy. Taking d again gives d(df)=(f_{yx}-f_{xy})\,dx\wedge dy. The equality of mixed partials says d(df)=0. This small cancellation will return later as the rule d^2=0. For now, it tells us why the Hessian of a smooth function is symmetric.

subsection.title: The Hessian matrix
subsection.p:
At a point (a,b), collect the second partials into one matrix:
H_f(a,b) = \begin{pmatrix} f_{xx}(a,b) \amp f_{xy}(a,b)\\ f_{yx}(a,b) \amp f_{yy}(a,b) \end{pmatrix}.

statement.p:
For h(x,y)=100+4x+3y-x^2-xy-y^2, we found h_{xx}=-2, \qquad h_{xy}=-1, \qquad h_{yx}=-1, \qquad h_{yy}=-2. So
H_h= \begin{pmatrix} -2 \amp -1\\ -1 \amp -2 \end{pmatrix}.
This Hessian is constant because h is already a quadratic function.

example.title: A Hessian that changes from point to point

statement.p:
Let f(x,y)=x^3-3x+y^2. Then f_x=3x^2-3, \qquad f_y=2y. So f_{xx}=6x, \qquad f_{xy}=0, \qquad f_{yx}=0, \qquad f_{yy}=2. The Hessian is
H_f(x,y) = \begin{pmatrix} 6x \amp 0\\ 0 \amp 2 \end{pmatrix}.
At the critical point (1,0),
H_f(1,0) = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \amp 0\\ 0 \amp 2 \end{pmatrix}.
At the critical point (-1,0),
H_f(-1,0) = \begin{pmatrix} -6 \amp 0\\ 0 \amp 2 \end{pmatrix}.
The same function bends differently at its two critical points.

subsection.p: The Hessian is the matrix version of the second derivative. To see this, we need the second-order approximation.

subsection.title: Quadratic approximation near a critical point
subsection.p:
In one-variable calculus, Taylor's formula said that near a, f(a+h)=f(a)+f'(a)h+\frac12f''(a)h^2+\text{smaller terms}. In two variables, the same story has more directions. Near (a,b), write x=a+u, \qquad y=b+v. Then the second-order approximation is
f(a+u,b+v) \amp\approx f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)u+f_y(a,b)v \amp\qquad +\frac12\left(f_{xx}(a,b)u^2+2f_{xy}(a,b)uv+f_{yy}(a,b)v^2\right).
The first line is the linear approximation. The second line is the quadratic correction.

example.title: A minimum seen through its quadratic part

statement.p:
Let f(x,y)=x^3-3x+y^2. At the critical point (1,0), write x=1+u, \qquad y=v. Then
f(1+u,v) \amp= (1+u)^3-3(1+u)+v^2 \amp= -2+3u^2+u^3+v^2.
The quadratic part is 3u^2+v^2. The Hessian at (1,0) is
H_f(1,0) = \begin{pmatrix} 6\amp 0\\ 0\amp 2 \end{pmatrix},
and
\frac12 \begin{pmatrix}u\amp v\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 6\amp 0\\ 0\amp 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}u\\v\end{pmatrix} = 3u^2+v^2.
So the Hessian gives exactly the quadratic part of the local expansion.

Suppose f(x,y) has continuous second partial derivatives near (a,b). Then
f(a+u,b+v) \amp= f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)u+f_y(a,b)v \amp\qquad +\frac12\left(f_{xx}(a,b)u^2+2f_{xy}(a,b)uv+f_{yy}(a,b)v^2\right)+R(u,v),
where the remainder R(u,v) is small compared with u^2+v^2:
\boxed{ \frac{R(u,v)}{u^2+v^2}\to0 \quad\text{as}\quad (u,v)\to(0,0). }
Equivalently,
\boxed{ f((a,b)+\mathbf u) = f(a,b) + df_{(a,b)}(\mathbf u) + \frac12\mathbf u^T H_f(a,b)\mathbf u + R(\mathbf u), }
with \frac{R(\mathbf u)}{\|\mathbf u\|^2}\to0.

proof.title: Proof idea
proof.p:
Move from (a,b) to (a+u,b+v) along the straight path \mathbf r(t)=(a,b)+t(u,v), \qquad 0\le t\le1. The composite g(t)=f(\mathbf r(t)) is a one-variable function. Taylor's formula for g at t=0 gives g(1)=g(0)+g'(0)+\frac12g''(0)+\text{smaller terms}. The chain rule gives g'(0)=df_{(a,b)}(u,v), and a second use of the chain rule gives
g''(0) = \begin{pmatrix}u\amp v\end{pmatrix} H_f(a,b) \begin{pmatrix}u\\v\end{pmatrix}.
This gives the formula. Continuity of the second partials is what makes the remaining terms smaller than \|\mathbf u\|^2.

subsection.p: At a critical point, the first-order part vanishes. The Hessian becomes the first visible shape. But a matrix is not yet an answer. We still need to know how to read \mathbf u^T H\mathbf u. Can it be positive in every nonzero direction? Negative in every direction? Positive in one direction and negative in another? Those three possibilities are the mechanism behind the second derivative test.
